vulnerability.title: Verify Permissions on shadow File
vulnerability.description: To properly set the permissions of /etc/shadow, run the command: $ sudo chmod 0000 /etc/shadow
vulnerability.group: Verify Permissions on Files with Local Account Information and Credentials

triage_decision.reason: The remediation only changes the mode of /etc/shadow to the standard STIG‑required 0000. Root can still read the file, and this is a common, low‑impact hardening step. No services need to be restarted and there is no risk of boot or kernel changes.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; Potential temporary authentication issues if applied incorrectly; Requires root privileges

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
We need to follow workflow: first PLAN description, then call review_plan. So we produce plan: check current permissions, then chmod to 0000 (which is actually 0000? typical /etc/shadow perms are 0o0000? Actually typical is 0o0000? Wait typical are 0400 (owner read). STIG likely 0000 with ACLs? But recommendation says chmod u-xwrs,g-xwrs,o-xwrt => that gives mode 0000 (no permissions for anyone). So plan: run stat -c %a /etc/shadow to verify, then chmod 0000.

We must not modify files directly, just permission.

Thus plan: run command chmod 0000 /etc/shadow.

We need to call review_plan with description.

review_feedback: Setting /etc/shadow to 0000 will prevent root and authentication services from reading the file, causing login failures and possible system lockout. The standard and secure permissions for /etc/shadow are typically 0640 (owner root read/write, group shadow read) or at minimum 0400 (owner root read only). Adjust ownership and group as required instead of removing all permissions.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: Setting /etc/shadow to 0000 will prevent root and authentication services from reading the file, causing login failures and possible system lockout. The standard and secure permissions for /etc/shadow are typically 0640 (owner root read/write, group shadow read) or at minimum 0400 (owner root read only). Adjust ownership and group as required instead of removing all permissions.
concerns: Root and PAM modules will be unable to read /etc/shadow, breaking authentication
System lockout risk for all users
Permissions deviate from conventional Linux best‑practice and may not meet actual STIG requirements
suggested_improvements: Ensure file owner is root and group is shadow
Validate the change with `ls -l /etc/shadow` and test login functionality
Document the rationale and reference the applicable security guideline
